title: Coconut macaroons
subtitle: British baked coconut macaroons with a chewy centre
excerpt: Mounds of desiccated coconut bound with egg white and sugar, baked until the exterior is golden and lightly crisp while the interior stays dense and chewy. A British tea-room standard, different from the French macaron in every way. Simple enough for a beginner, done in 30 minutes.
sourceNotes: The British coconut macaroon, a baked mound of desiccated coconut, egg white, and sugar, is a standard of the British tea-room and domestic baking tradition, documented in British domestic cookbooks throughout the twentieth century. It is distinct from the French macaron, which is a meringue-and-almond-flour sandwich. The recipe here synthesises the established British domestic formula for baked coconut macaroons.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze individually once cool. Thaw at room temperature for 30 minutes.
recipe.batchNotes: Doubles straightforwardly. Bake in batches on two trays.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Keep in a tin for up to four days. The exterior softens slightly after day one.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: conventional oven. Fan at 150°C. Macaroons should be pale gold all over — deep golden at the base and on any exposed tips, lighter on the mounded sides. They firm as they cool.

glossaryTerms.definition: The British coconut macaroon is a baked mound of desiccated coconut held together by whisked egg white and sugar. It is baked until the exterior is lightly crisp and golden while the interior remains dense and moist. It is entirely different from the French macaron, which is a meringue-and-almond sandwich. If the macaroon falls apart when baked, the egg whites were not whisked enough to bind the coconut.

content.text: depends on the egg whites being whisked to soft peaks before mixing in the coconut — they need enough body to hold the mound together in the oven. Do not beat to stiff peaks, which makes the mixture dry and the macaroon fragile. Soft to medium peaks give the right result.

Heat the oven to 170°C conventional. Line a baking tray with baking paper. Whisk the egg whites with the salt to soft peaks. Add the sugar and vanilla and whisk briefly until combined. Fold in the desiccated coconut until the mixture is evenly moistened — it should hold its shape when scooped.

Scoop heaped tablespoons of the mixture onto the lined tray, spacing them 4 cm apart. Press each mound lightly to compact it, shaping it into a rough dome. Bake for 18 to 20 minutes until pale gold all over — deeper colour at the base and on any protruding tips, lighter on the body. They will feel soft when pressed but firm up as they cool. Leave on the tray for 10 minutes before transferring to a rack.
